schools.summary: Paul's solemn forewarning: 'this know also, that in the last days (the latter times of the present age, stretching to Christ's return) perilous (chalepoi — dangerous, hard, grievous, fierce) times shall come' — Timothy to know that seasons of moral danger and difficulty will mark the age; 'the last days' embracing the whole gospel age (the days of the Messiah) with special reference to periods of declension within it, the perils already beginning and to recur and intensify (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: the times are 'perilous' not chiefly from persecution but from the corruption of morals and religion he proceeds to describe (vv.2-5); the danger is to faith and holiness, the moral peril of the age.

themes: This know also, that in the last days perilous times shall come
the warning of perilous times in the last days
dangerous times of moral corruption
the perils of the latter age

schools.summary: The list of corruptions opens with the root: 'men shall be lovers of their own selves (philautoi — self-lovers, selfish), covetous (philargyroi — lovers of money)' — selfishness and greed heading the catalogue as the springs of the rest; 'boasters (braggarts), proud (haughty, arrogant), blasphemers (railers, evil-speakers against God or men)' — the swelling of pride and the abuse of the tongue; 'disobedient to parents (rebellious), unthankful (ungrateful), unholy (impure, profane, without reverence)' — the collapse of the most basic bonds of duty, gratitude, and reverence (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: men shall be lovers of their own selves, covetous, boasters, proud, blasphemers
disobedient to parents, unthankful, unholy
the catalogue of vices beginning with self-love
the moral corruption of the last days

schools.summary: The next cluster: 'without natural affection (astorgoi — devoid of family love, hard-hearted toward kin), trucebreakers (aspondoi — implacable, covenant-breaking, irreconcilable), false accusers (diaboloi — slanderers, devils)' — the absence of natural love and the malice of slander; 'incontinent (akrateis — without self-control, intemperate), fierce (anemeroi — savage, brutal, untamed), despisers of those that are good (aphilagathoi — haters of good and of good men)' — the loss of all self-mastery, the rise of brutal savagery, and enmity against goodness itself (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: Without natural affection, trucebreakers, false accusers, incontinent, fierce
despisers of those that are good
the loss of affection and self-control
savagery and enmity against good

schools.summary: The next vices: 'traitors (prodotai — betrayers, treacherous), heady (propeteis — rash, reckless, headstrong), highminded (tetyphomenoi — puffed up, conceited, beclouded with pride)' — treachery, recklessness, and swollen conceit; the climactic vice 'lovers of pleasures (philedonoi) more than lovers of God (philotheoi)' — the great antithesis, pleasure dethroning God in the heart, men loving their pleasures above their Maker, the deepest mark of the corruption; the catalogue framed by self-love (v.2) and pleasure-love, God displaced throughout (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Traitors, heady, highminded, lovers of pleasures more than lovers of God
the climax: pleasure-lovers rather than God-lovers
pleasure dethroning God in the heart
the deepest root of corruption

schools.summary: The crowning trait of these corrupt men: 'having a form (morphosis — outward shape, appearance, semblance) of godliness, but denying (renouncing, repudiating in practice) the power thereof' — keeping the outward show of religion while denying its inward, transforming power, a hollow, hypocritical religion; the 'power of godliness' being its real, inward efficacy, the grace that renews and sanctifies, which their lives deny has any hold on them; 'from such turn away (apotrepou — avoid, shun, keep clear of)' — Timothy to separate himself from such hypocrites (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Having a form of godliness, but denying the power thereof
from such turn away
the form of godliness without its power
turning away from hypocrites

schools.summary: The description of these false teachers' tactics: 'of this sort are they which creep into (worm their way into, insinuate themselves into) houses (households), and lead captive (take captive, enslave) silly women (gynaikaria — weak, foolish little women)' — gaining entrance by stealth and preying on the spiritually weak; 'laden with sins (heaped with guilt, burdened with an evil conscience)' — women weighed down by sin and a troubled conscience, easy prey; 'led away (driven, controlled) with divers (various) lusts' — ruled by changing desires and passions, making them susceptible to seduction, their own lusts delivering them into the deceivers' hands (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: they which creep into houses, and lead captive silly women laden with sins
led away with divers lusts
the deceivers who prey on weak women
insinuating false teachers

schools.summary: The pitiable state of these captive women: 'ever learning (always being taught, forever taking up new instruction), and never able to come to the knowledge (epignosin — full knowledge) of the truth' — perpetually occupied with novelty, yet never arriving at real, saving truth; always seeking, always listening to new teachers and ideas but with no settled grasp, their learning endless motion without arrival, curiosity without conviction; 'never able' not from lack of opportunity but from their own unstable, lust-ruled condition (v.6), the heart enslaved to sin unable to reach the truth however much it hears (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Ever learning, and never able to come to the knowledge of the truth
the deceived ever learning but never knowing truth
novelty without conviction
the enslaved heart unable to reach truth

schools.summary: The comparison drawn from Jewish tradition: 'now as Jannes and Jambres (the names of the Egyptian magicians who opposed Moses before Pharaoh, Exodus 7) withstood (resisted, opposed) Moses' — the magicians who imitated Moses's signs to oppose God's truth; 'so do these also resist (oppose, set themselves against) the truth' — the false teachers counterfeiting and resisting the gospel as the magicians did Moses; 'men of corrupt (depraved, debased) minds, reprobate (adokimoi — rejected, failing the test, counterfeit) concerning the faith' — their understanding corrupted and, tested by the faith, found counterfeit and rejected (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: as Jannes and Jambres withstood Moses, so do these also resist the truth
men of corrupt minds, reprobate concerning the faith
the deceivers resisting truth like Jannes and Jambres
counterfeit and rejected

schools.summary: The comfort: 'but they shall proceed no further (advance no more, be stopped)' — the false teachers' success has a limit, God checking their progress so that they will not prevail in the end; 'for their folly (anoia — madness, senselessness) shall be manifest (made plain, exposed) unto all men' — their deceit and error openly exposed, their pretensions unmasked before all; 'as theirs (Jannes and Jambres) also was' — as the Egyptian magicians were finally exposed and confounded, unable to match Moses's later signs (Exodus 8:18-19), so these deceivers will be unmasked, the precedent guaranteeing the outcome (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: but they shall proceed no further: for their folly shall be manifest unto all men
as theirs also was
the deceivers' folly exposed and progress checked
the limit of error

schools.summary: The turn from the deceivers to Timothy: 'but thou hast fully known (parekolouthesas — closely followed, traced out, been a close witness to) my doctrine (teaching), manner of life (conduct, way of living)' — over against the false teachers, Timothy had intimately known both Paul's teaching and his life; the catalogue of graces, answering the catalogue of vices (vv.2-4): 'purpose (his aim, resolve), faith, longsuffering (patient endurance of wrong), charity (love), patience (steadfast endurance)' — the graces that marked Paul's whole life, observed by Timothy as a close, long-term companion (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: thou hast fully known my doctrine, manner of life, purpose, faith, longsuffering, charity, patience
Timothy's full knowledge of Paul's doctrine and life
the pattern of graces over against the vices
a close witness of teaching and life

schools.summary: Paul recalls his sufferings, which Timothy had known: 'persecutions, afflictions, which came unto me at Antioch (Pisidian), at Iconium, at Lystra' — the cities of Paul's first journey (Acts 13-14), where he was opposed, driven out, and at Lystra stoned, places near Timothy's home and well known to him; 'what persecutions I endured (bore, held out under)' — not merely that he suffered but that he endured, his constancy part of the example; 'but out of them all the Lord delivered (rescued) me' — through every persecution the Lord brought him safely, the faithful sufferer not abandoned but delivered (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: persecutions, afflictions, which came unto me at Antioch, at Iconium, at Lystra
but out of them all the Lord delivered me
the persecutions Paul endured and the Lord's deliverance
suffering and rescue

schools.summary: Paul draws a general law from his own case: 'yea, and all that will live godly (eusebos — piously, devoutly) in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution (be persecuted)' — not Paul only but all who are resolved to live a godly life in union with Christ will meet persecution, the common lot of the faithful; 'will live godly' meaning those determined and resolved to live devoutly, the godly life itself by its contrast with the world provoking opposition, holiness drawing persecution as light exposes darkness; the cross belonging to the Christian life (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: all that will live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution
the general truth that the godly will suffer
holiness provoking the world's hostility
the cross of the godly

schools.summary: The contrast with the persecuted godly: 'but evil men and seducers (goetes — impostors, sorcerers, deceivers) shall wax worse and worse (proceed from bad to worse, advance in evil)' — the wicked, far from improving, progressing in corruption, evil being self-deepening; 'deceiving (others), and being deceived (themselves)' — the twofold ruin, both leading others astray and themselves led astray, the deceiver also a dupe caught in his own delusion; a warning of deepening corruption among the wicked (the godly suffering while the evil grow worse), not a promise of the world's improvement (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: But evil men and seducers shall wax worse and worse, deceiving, and being deceived
evil men growing worse, deceiving and deceived
the deceiver his own dupe
deepening corruption of the wicked

schools.summary: Paul's charge over against the worsening deceivers: 'but continue thou (abide, remain, hold fast) in the things which thou hast learned (been taught)' — Timothy to stay steadfastly in the sound doctrine he received, not carried away by the prevailing error; 'and hast been assured of (been convinced of, made fully sure of)' — not merely learned but inwardly persuaded, settled conviction anchoring him; 'knowing of whom (from whom) thou hast learned them' — from Paul himself and from his godly mother and grandmother (1:5), the trustworthiness of his teachers a ground of his confidence (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: But continue thou in the things which thou hast learned and hast been assured of
knowing of whom thou hast learned them
the charge to continue in what he learned
confident in trustworthy teachers

schools.summary: Timothy's lifelong knowledge of Scripture: 'and that from a child (from infancy, from his earliest years) thou hast known the holy scriptures (hiera grammata — the sacred writings, the Old Testament)' — taught by his godly mother and grandmother (1:5), Timothy had known the Scriptures from earliest childhood; they 'are able to make thee wise unto salvation' — to impart the saving wisdom, to lead to and instruct in the way of salvation, sufficient to make one wise unto eternal life; 'through faith which is in Christ Jesus' — this saving wisdom unlocked not by bare reading but as the Scriptures lead to faith in Christ, faith being the key to their saving power (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: from a child thou hast known the holy scriptures, which are able to make thee wise unto salvation
through faith which is in Christ Jesus
the Scriptures known from childhood
saving wisdom through faith in Christ

schools.summary: Barnes (with Gill, Chrysostom, Wesley) follows the received reading (the predicate): 'all scripture IS given by inspiration of God (theopneustos — God-breathed), AND IS profitable' — a positive assertion that all Scripture is inspired, then profitable; Chrysostom: 'all such is given by inspiration of God; therefore do not doubt.' The word theopneustos means God-breathed, breathed out by God as his own utterance, as truly traced to him as the breath of life — asserting the Scriptures' divine origin. Robertson holds both renderings grammatically possible (the missing article before graphe and the absent copula leaving it open), but notes the predicate reading gives 'a definite assertion of inspiration.' Profitable for the fourfold work: 'doctrine (teaching truth), reproof (convicting of error and sin), correction (epanorthosis — setting right, reformation), and instruction in righteousness (training in right living)' — the complete equipment for forming the believer.

Ellicott and Clarke prefer the attributive rendering (with the Revised Version, the Syriac and Vulgate versions, and many fathers — Origen, Theodoret, Grotius, Luther): 'Every scripture inspired by God is ALSO profitable' — taking 'inspired by God' as describing 'scripture' and 'profitable' as the predicate, since (they argue) Timothy and the trained Jew needed no proof that the Old Testament was inspired, and nothing in the context calls for such an assertion. Clarke notes the 'and' (kai) is omitted by many versions and fathers; the verse then exhorts Timothy to test his teaching by every inspired writing. (Even so, both affirm the Scriptures' divine inspiration; the difference is grammatical, not a denial of inspiration.)

debates.question: How should 'All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable' be rendered?

sides.position: The predicate reading (AV): 'All Scripture IS inspired AND profitable' — a positive assertion of inspiration
The attributive reading (RV): 'Every inspired Scripture is ALSO profitable' — 'inspired' describing Scripture, 'profitable' the predicate
holders: the Syriac, Vulgate, and many fathers (Origen, Theodoret, Luther)

sides.position: Both grammatically possible (missing article and copula), the predicate reading giving a definite assertion of inspiration

themes: All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness
the inspiration and profit of all Scripture
the translation crux: predicate or attributive
God-breathed and profitable

schools.summary: The end of Scripture's profit (v.16): 'that the man of God (the believer, especially the minister) may be perfect (artios — complete, fitted, fully developed, lacking nothing)' — the design of the inspired, profitable Scripture to make the man of God complete in every part; 'the man of God' the one devoted to God (Timothy as minister, and every servant of God), formed and equipped by the Scriptures; 'throughly furnished (exertismenos — fully equipped, completely fitted out, as a workman with all his tools) unto all good works' — Scripture supplying all that is needed for every good work, the man of God lacking nothing for his task, a witness to the sufficiency of Scripture (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: That the man of God may be perfect, throughly furnished unto all good works
the Scriptures' aim: to make the man of God complete
fully equipped for every good work
the sufficiency of Scripture
